content: The application follows a modular architecture where each major CLI command is organized as a separate module under `/src/modules/`.
The Core Modules are auth - Authentication (login/logout), config - Configuration management (build/push/pull), info - Information queries (info/list), health - Health checks and diagnostics.
Command Layer Purpose: CLI interface layer - handles user input, validation, and output formatting.

Command Layer Responsibilities: Parse CLI arguments and options using nest-commander, Validate user input, Call appropriate service methods, Format and display output to user, Handle CLI-specific errors and help messages.
Command Layer Naming Convention: `<action>.command.ts`.
Service Layer Purpose: Business logic layer - contains core application functionality.

Service Layer Responsibilities: Implement business logic and rules, Handle data transformation and validation, Manage external API calls (Supabase, file system), Coordinate between different services, Handle business-level error scenarios.
Service Layer Naming Convention: `<domain>.service.ts`.
Each module must Export a NestJS module class that imports all commands and services.
Each module must Register commands with nest-commander.
Each module must Provide all services through dependency injection.
Each module must Import shared services from `/shared/`.
Dependency Flow is CLI Input → Command Layer → Service Layer → External APIs/File System and User Output ← Business Logic ← Data Processing.
Services can depend on other services through dependency injection.
Commands should only depend on services within their module or shared services.
Use shared interfaces in `/shared/interfaces/` for type definitions.
Shared utilities go in `/shared/utils/`.
Mirror the module structure in tests: `src/modules/auth/commands/login.command.spec.ts` and `src/modules/auth/services/auth.service.spec.ts`.
Models Layer Purpose: Data models and type definitions for core business entities.

Models Layer Responsibilities: Define TypeScript interfaces and classes for business entities, Provide type safety across the application, Define data validation rules and constraints, Map between external API responses and internal data structures.
Models Layer Naming Convention: `<entity>.model.ts`.

Core Models: `config-bundle.model.ts` - Configuration bundle structure and metadata, `user.model.ts` - User profile and authentication data, `sync-session.model.ts` - Synchronization session tracking, `ide-settings.model.ts` - IDE-specific settings and preferences.
File Naming Conventions for Commands: `<action>.command.ts`.
File Naming Conventions for Services: `<domain>.service.ts`.
File Naming Conventions for Modules: `<module>.module.ts`.
File Naming Conventions for DTOs: `<domain>.dto.ts`.
File Naming Conventions for Models: `<entity>.model.ts`.
File Naming Conventions for Interfaces: `<domain>.interface.ts`.
File Naming Conventions for Tests: `<filename>.spec.ts`.
Use barrel exports (`index.ts`) for clean imports between modules.
Import from `@/modules/<module>` using path aliases.
Models: `@/models/<entity>.model`.
Shared utilities: `@/shared/<type>/<name>`.
External dependencies at the top, internal imports below.
Import Order: External dependencies (npm packages), Models (`@/models/`), Shared utilities (`@/shared/`), Module-specific imports (`@/modules/`), Relative imports (`./`, `../`).
This architecture ensures clear separation of concerns, testability, and maintainability while following NestJS best practices.
